title: Intel's Centrino: Don't Man the Barricades
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen Group opinion piece by Peter S. Kastner examining Intel's Centrino platform launch on March 12, 2003. Centrino integrated a mobile Pentium 4, chipset, and 802.11 wireless adapter into a unified mobile platform backed by a $300M marketing campaign. Kastner argues against the prevalent IT practice of banning wireless notebooks due to security concerns over rogue access points, noting over 25% of business Centrino notebooks shipped with wireless radios removed. He advocates embracing wireless with available security solutions (Cisco LEAP, Windows Server 2003 hardening) citing PricewaterhouseCoopers ROI data of 6 months payback and Aberdeen research showing 30 extra productive minutes per day for meeting attendees.
importance_rationale: Captured a pivotal inflection point in enterprise wireless adoption centered on Intel's most important 2003 product launch with a $300M marketing spend that fundamentally changed mobile computing.
relevance_rationale: The tension between wireless productivity gains and enterprise security concerns identified in 2003 remains a central theme in IT policy today with BYOD and zero-trust networking.
prescience_rationale: Kastner correctly predicted that banning wireless was futile and counterproductive. Wi-Fi became ubiquitous in enterprise environments within 5 years. The productivity gains he cited proved conservative as wireless transformed not just campus computing but entire work paradigms.
